segments.title: Gorshkov builds Soviet surface power through destroyers
segments.summary: The opening explains why Project 56 destroyers gave Admiral Sergei Gorshkov a practical route from a submarine-centered fleet toward missile-armed Soviet surface power.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts Cold War memories of submarines and cruisers with the much larger everyday role of destroyers. Project 56 ships gave Gorshkov hull numbers, upgradeable compartments, and a politically acceptable way to introduce surface-to-air missiles while making the case for larger warships. Their patrol and presence work also demonstrated something submarines could not provide without revealing themselves.

evidence.note: The introduction identifies destroyers as numerous and unusually important Cold War surface combatants.
Project 56 construction and compartmentalized modernization let Gorshkov add missiles and build surface-fleet capability.
Surface ships are presented as adaptable, visible instruments for patrols, task groups, and peacetime presence.

segments.title: Kildin destroyers turn missiles into fleet roles
segments.summary: The Kildin class shows Soviet destroyers evolving from gun-and-torpedo ships into guided-missile strike and anti-submarine platforms.
segments.body: The Project 56 lineage becomes a design philosophy rather than an unchanging hull, with anti-ship missiles adding a strike role while newer torpedoes improve anti-submarine warfare. Dr. Clarke argues that a submarine-heavy Soviet doctrine naturally made enemy submarines a priority threat. These small classes mattered less for their final numbers than for the technologies and operational ideas they carried into the emerging blue-water fleet.

evidence.note: The Kildin evolution adds anti-ship missiles and shifts the destroyer toward guided-missile strike.
Improved homing torpedoes, Soviet ASW priorities, and the class's value as a blue-water stepping stone are explained.

segments.title: Cuba exposes the Soviet surface-fleet gap
segments.summary: The Cuban Missile Crisis reveals why submarines alone could not escort merchant ships, signal resolve, contest the air, or give Moscow a convincing visible fleet presence.
segments.body: Soviet submarines possessed serious combat power, but protecting the Cuba-bound shipping visibly would have required them to expose themselves or use weapons. Without large surface escorts, Moscow could not place ships between the convoy and the blockade, contest American aviation, or stage a persuasive fleet demonstration. Dr. Clarke uses the episode to argue for a balanced fleet whose different components prevent an opponent from countering one concentrated strength.

evidence.note: The crisis demonstrates the escort, signaling, and diplomatic limits of a fleet centered on submarines and small craft.
The inability to project visible power and contest the air leads into the argument for balanced fleet capabilities.

segments.title: Kanin and Kashin develop the Soviet destroyer template
segments.summary: Kanin conversions and the purpose-built Kashin class establish a multi-role Soviet destroyer pattern of missiles, guns, ASW weapons, sensors, and deliberate upgrade paths.
segments.body: Kanin-class ships move from large anti-ship missile batteries toward air-defense and anti-submarine duties as weapon technology changes. The Kashins then combine anti-ship, anti-aircraft, and ASW systems from the outset, creating the recognizable Soviet surface-combatant template. Their crowded layout and short endurance expose the limits of fitting every desired capability into a modest hull, strengthening Gorshkov's case for larger ships.

evidence.note: The Kanins are described as adaptable ships whose role shifts from anti-ship strike toward air defense and ASW.
The Kashin class introduces a purpose-built combination of anti-ship, anti-air, and anti-submarine weapons.
The crowded arrangement and ten-day endurance illustrate both the design's capability and its limits.

segments.title: Bastion defense drives a new Soviet task group
segments.summary: Kiev carriers and Soviet bastion doctrine create a demand for destroyers able to defend larger task groups and contest the air around submarine operating areas.
segments.body: Bastion defense required Soviet forces to protect ballistic-missile and diesel submarines while drawing NATO attention and strength elsewhere. Kiev's fighters could contest the air while land-based bombers supplied much of the strike power, but the carrier still needed a capable escort group. Exercises showed that the older Kashins lacked the endurance and combat capacity expected for warfare in the late 1970s and 1980s, prompting a much larger destroyer generation.

evidence.note: The discussion links global diversion, bastion defense, Kiev's arrival, and the inadequacy of existing destroyers.
Kiev's defensive aviation role and the need to contest airspace for submarine operations are laid out.
The carrier's escort needs lead directly to the 1976 laying down of the Sovremenny class.

segments.title: Sovremenny combines strike, air defense, and presence
segments.summary: The Sovremenny class makes a major leap in displacement, Sunburn anti-ship reach, Gadfly air defense, gun power, aviation support, and visible status.
segments.body: At roughly 8,000 tons fully loaded, Sovremenny is far larger than its predecessors and carries 130 mm guns, Sunburn anti-ship missiles, Gadfly surface-to-air missiles, ASW weapons, and a helicopter facility. The design aims to fight as a flexible escort and surface-strike platform while supporting ships around it. Its cleaner silhouette and long service also make the class a visible demonstration of Soviet shipbuilding capability, not merely a utilitarian combatant.

evidence.note: The class's displacement jump, ASW fit, helicopter support, and general-purpose guns are introduced.
Sunburn and Gadfly missile capabilities provide anti-ship strike and task-group air defense.
The class is described as a leap in operational and status capability with durable general-purpose value.

segments.title: Destroyer mass supports Soviet cruiser prestige
segments.summary: Soviet cruisers receive prestige and leading-edge investment, while destroyers supply the fleet's numbers, test new systems, and sustain the formation's real strength.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke links conservative propulsion choices to the importance of visible reliability: a surface ship used to project Soviet power could not afford a conspicuous breakdown. Cruisers gained more attention and often received mature technology first, but relatively few were completed. Destroyers carried the fleet's mass, tested concepts, and remained essential in task groups and bastion defense even as many hulls aged.

evidence.note: Conservative machinery choices are tied to Gorshkov's caution and the need for reliable visible presence.
Cruiser building totals and investment are contrasted with destroyers' numerical and experimental role.
Older destroyers remain in task groups and bastion defense because the fleet still depends on them.

segments.title: Udaloy and Sovremenny work as an interdependent team
segments.summary: Udaloy and Sovremenny destroyers form complementary Soviet task-group partners through shared aviation support, layered missile threats, ASW weapons, and bastion mine warfare.
segments.body: The Udaloy's hangars and SS-N-14 weapon give it strong ASW reach without eliminating its anti-surface threat, while Sovremenny contributes Sunburn missiles and broader surface-combat power. Operating together, the classes can share helicopter support and combine missile profiles that complicate an opponent's defenses. Mine rails, submarines, and coordinated escorts reinforce a Soviet preference for interdependent battle groups, though the post-Cold War Russian fleet struggled to sustain that model.

evidence.note: The Udaloy's hangars and SS-N-14 missile-torpedo system are assessed as both ASW and anti-ship capabilities.
Mine warfare, shared helicopter support, and combined Sovremenny-Udaloy missile attacks illustrate task-group interdependence.
The conclusion returns to destroyers as fleet muscle and traces the Udaloy legacy into post-Cold War Russian service.
